one_line_takeaway: Complex-valued neural networks are often as robust or more robust than real-valued ones against standard adversarial attacks, but both are highly vulnerable to a new phase-only 'Phase Attack' that perturbs just the (rarely visualized, hard-to-inspect) phase channel of complex-valued inputs -- sometimes more effectively than unrestricted attacks.
key_finding: (1) CVNNs are generally on par with or more robust than RVNNs against unrestricted adversarial attacks, across both datasets, both architectures, and with/without adversarial training -- with one clear exception (ConvNeXt on the Prostate dataset without adversarial training, where the RVNN is more robust); the paper separately notes two experiments overall in which the RVNN outperforms the CVNN. In several cases even the weakest attack (CFGSM) degrades the RVNN more than the strongest attack (CIFGSM) degrades the CVNN. (2) Both CVNNs and RVNNs are highly susceptible to phase-only perturbation: Magnitude Attacks are consistently the weakest of the three attack types, while Phase Attacks are comparable to, and for multi-step variants (PIFGSM, PMIFGSM) sometimes stronger than, the unrestricted attacks (CIFGSM, CMIFGSM) -- despite Phase Attacks being a strictly more constrained (theoretically weaker) subset of the unrestricted search space. On PolSAR, the multi-step Phase Attacks fully zero out F1 on all tasks at perturbation bound epsilon >= 0.01, a level at which the unrestricted attacks never fully succeed; on Prostate, both Phase and unrestricted attacks sometimes reach F1 = 0 already at epsilon = 0.005, and the ResNet on Prostate shows a sharp normalized-F1 drop after epsilon = 0.0001. Single-step Phase Attacks (PFGSM, PFFGSM), by contrast, are weaker than their unrestricted counterparts (CFGSM, CFFGSM). The authors interpret the surprising multi-step result as the phase restriction making the iterative optimization landscape easier to navigate to a good local optimum, not as evidence the restricted attack is fundamentally stronger in a worst-case sense. Adversarial training reduces but does not eliminate this susceptibility, and its benefit transfers to Phase/Magnitude Attacks even though training only used unrestricted attacks.
core_contribution: The paper (1) derives complex-valued analogues of four standard gradient-based adversarial attacks (FGSM, FFGSM, IFGSM/PGD, MIFGSM -> CFGSM, CFFGSM, CIFGSM, CMIFGSM) using Wirtinger calculus so they apply to complex-valued neural network (CVNN) inputs; (2) introduces a new attack family, the Phase Attack (PFGSM/PFFGSM/PIFGSM/PMIFGSM), which is constrained to perturb only the phase component of a complex-valued input while leaving the magnitude untouched, plus a complementary Magnitude Attack that perturbs only the magnitude while leaving phase fixed; and (3) uses these attacks to run the first comparative empirical study of adversarial robustness between real-valued neural networks (RVNNs) and CVNNs on genuinely complex-valued input data (as opposed to real-valued images with a Fourier-domain phase). The authors also release a PyTorch toolbox implementing all presented attacks for the complex domain.
threat_model: White-box, gradient-based adversarial perturbation of model inputs at inference time, formalized as a bounded L-infinity optimization problem (perturb Z within an epsilon-ball of clean input X to maximize a loss l(f(Z),Y)). The paper extends this to the complex domain (Z, X in C^n) via Wirtinger derivatives, and additionally defines two restricted variants: the Phase Attack (perturbation confined to the phase angle of each complex pixel, magnitude held constant) and the Magnitude Attack (perturbation confined to magnitude, phase held constant). The motivating premise, stated by the authors, is that phase information in complex-valued images (e.g., MRI, PolSAR/radar) is rarely visualized and is noisy/hard for a human to interpret, so phase-only perturbations are especially likely to go unnoticed -- making Phase Attacks a realistic 'stealthy' attack surface for safety-critical CVNN deployments such as MRI reconstruction/diagnosis and autonomous driving/radar.
limitations: Results are read off epsilon-sweep plots (Fig. 3, Fig. 4) rather than reported in tables, so precision beyond what the text states in prose is not available from this source. Only two datasets (PolSAR, FastMRI Prostate) and two architectures (ResNet, ConvNeXt) are evaluated; the authors themselves flag this as a hypothesis to be 'tested on a wider scale' in future work. All CVNNs are initialized from pretrained RVNN weights via the authors' own prior EQ method, which could interact with the robustness comparison. The comparative robustness finding is explicitly non-uniform (RVNN wins in at least the ConvNeXt/Prostate/no-adversarial-training setting, and the paper separately states two experiments where RVNN is more robust), so the 'CVNNs are more robust' claim is a trend with named exceptions, not a universal result. Only gradient-sign-based attack families (FGSM/PGD/momentum lineage) are adapted and tested; other attack classes (e.g., optimization-based C&W-style, black-box/transfer attacks) are not evaluated. No formal certified-robustness or Lipschitz-bound guarantees are derived for the new attacks (unlike the cited but distinct theoretical work of Neacsu et al.).

datasets_used: FastMRI Prostate (biparametric MRI, preprocessed per Rempe et al. 2024 k-space tumor-likelihood pipeline)
quantitative_results: PolSAR: multi-step Phase Attacks (PIFGSM, PMIFGSM) reduce normalized F1 to 0 on all tasks at perturbation bound epsilon >= 0.01, whereas unrestricted attacks (CIFGSM, CMIFGSM) never reach F1 = 0 at any tested epsilon
Prostate: both Phase and unrestricted attacks sometimes drive normalized F1 to 0 already at epsilon = 0.005
Prostate ResNet: normalized F1 shows a sharp drop after epsilon = 0.0001 for both RVNN and CVNN under unrestricted attacks
PolSAR ResNet/ConvNeXt without adversarial training: CVNNs more robust than RVNNs at all tested noise levels (both begin degrading around epsilon = 0.005, RVNN degrades faster)
ConvNeXt on Prostate (without adversarial training) is the one architecture/dataset combination where RVNN robustness exceeds CVNN robustness across almost all noise levels
Single-step Phase Attacks (PFGSM, PFFGSM) are consistently less effective than their unrestricted counterparts (CFGSM, CFFGSM) on both models and both datasets
Magnitude Attacks are consistently the least effective of the three restriction types (unrestricted, Phase, Magnitude) across both datasets
baselines_compared: FFGSM (Faster FGSM, Wong et al.) / complex-valued CFFGSM
IFGSM / PGD (iterative, Madry et al.) / complex-valued CIFGSM
MIFGSM (momentum iterative, Dong et al.) / complex-valued CMIFGSM
RVNN vs. CVNN ResNet and ConvNeXt architectures, with and without adversarial training (Madry-style)
